sessions.overview:
# Wire --coverage-line into the bpu Makefile. Add per-module
# cov_* targets and a cov_bpu unit-level merge target.
# Output lands in rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/.
# Verify lcov and genhtml are in PATH before proceeding.
sessions.ia_output:
● INFRA-003 complete. Here's the summary:
                                                                                      
  Step 1 (tool check): verilator_coverage, lcov, genhtml all found in PATH. 
   
  Step 2-4 (Makefile): Added cov_history, cov_ubtb, cov_loop_pred, 
  cov_tage_table,
  cov_tage, cov_bpu, and all clean_cov_* targets to the BPU Makefile.
                                                                                       
  Key implementation detail discovered: Verilator 5.020's --binary-generated main()    
  does not call coveragep()->write(), and the +verilator+coverage+file+ runtime
  argument is not implemented. Solution: two-pass compile — --binary produces the      
  link-capable Vtb.mk, then sed patches Vtb__main.cpp to append the coverage write
  call, then make -C obj_dir -f Vtb.mk recompiles only the changed main. The binary is
  run via cd coverage/<module> && <abs_path>/Vtb so coverage.dat lands in the right
  directory.

  Step 5 (verification): All six HTML reports confirmed. Full regression (make all)    
  exits zero with zero warnings.
                                                                                       
  Coverage rates:                        
  - cov_history: 100.0%
  - cov_ubtb: 92.8%   
  - cov_loop_pred: 97.7%
  - cov_tage_table: 75.5%
  - cov_tage: 70.1%     
  - cov_bpu (merged): 76.5%

sessions.pa_assessment:
Good result overall. Here's my assessment:
The good:

All 6 HTML reports generated, all targets green, regression clean
Coverage rates are useful baselines: history 100%, ubtb 92.8%, loop_pred 97.7%, tage_table 75.5%, tage 70.1%, merged bpu 76.5%
The two-pass compile workaround for Verilator 5.020's missing coveragep()->write() is clever and contained to build artifacts — no source files touched

On the lib/ directory issue:
Looking at the prompt and results, I don't see evidence that files actually landed in rtl/core/frontend/bpu/lib/. The results show all HTML at coverage/*/html/index.html as specified. My best guess is that genhtml reported source annotation paths containing lib/ because the merged cov_bpu report pulls in coverage from lib components (bw_ram, sat_alu, dual_lm1 etc.) that tage and tage_table depend on. That would explain why you saw lib/ paths in the output — it's source annotation in the HTML, not actual file writes to lib/. Does that match what you saw?

Two items worth adding to debt:

cov_tage at 70.1% — tage_cntrl error paths uncovered, noted in Deferred Work, but worth a formal debt entry
genhtml absolute path prefix issue for CI environments

sessions.prompt_raw:
## Task ID
INFRA-003

## Context Loaded
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/Makefile

## Hypothesis
Adding --coverage-line to per-module sim targets and
introducing cov_* Makefile targets will produce line and
branch coverage reports for each BPU module via
verilator_coverage and genhtml. A cov_bpu target merges
all per-module coverage data into a single unit-level
report. This infrastructure will serve as the template
for other units when needed.

## Background
Verilator 5.020 supports --coverage-line (line and branch
coverage) and --coverage-user (cover property statements).
verilator_coverage can write .info files consumable by
genhtml to produce HTML reports. lcov and genhtml are
installed system-wide as Ubuntu 22.04 deb packages.

Per-module coverage targets follow the existing naming
convention: cov_tage, cov_loop_pred, cov_tage_table,
cov_history, cov_ubtb. A cov_bpu target merges all
per-module coverage.dat files and generates a combined
HTML report.

Coverage output directory structure:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/
    tage/          per-module coverage.dat and HTML
    loop_pred/     per-module coverage.dat and HTML
    tage_table/    per-module coverage.dat and HTML
    history/       per-module coverage.dat and HTML
    ubtb/          per-module coverage.dat and HTML
    bpu/           merged coverage.dat and HTML

## Binding Previous Decisions
- RVA_ROOT is always set externally. Never define it
  inside any Makefile. Use $(RVA_ROOT) for absolute
  paths where needed.
- Existing sim targets (sim_tage, sim_tage_fast,
  sim_history, sim_ubtb, sim_loop_pred, sim_tage_table)
  must not be modified. Coverage targets are additive.
- All targets must exit zero with zero warnings after
  existing suppressions.
- -Wno-PINMISSING is retained in lint_tage, sim_tage,
  sim_tage_fast per BP-023b/BP-023c decisions.

## Specific Requirements

### Step 1 -- Tool check

Before making any Makefile changes, verify that the
following tools are available in PATH:
  verilator_coverage
  lcov
  genhtml

If any tool is missing, report which tool is missing
and stop. Do not proceed with Makefile changes.

### Step 2 -- Per-module coverage targets

Add the following targets to the bpu Makefile. Each
target:
  1. Runs the corresponding sim binary with
     --coverage-line added to the Verilator compile
     flags, producing a coverage.dat in the module's
     coverage output directory.
  2. Runs verilator_coverage --write-info to convert
     coverage.dat to a .info file.
  3. Runs genhtml to produce an HTML report in the
     module's coverage subdirectory.
  4. Prints a summary line on completion.

Targets to add:
  cov_tage        feeds from sim_tage stimulus
  cov_tage_table  feeds from sim_tage_table stimulus
  cov_loop_pred   feeds from sim_loop_pred stimulus
  cov_history     feeds from sim_history stimulus
  cov_ubtb        feeds from sim_ubtb stimulus

Each target compiles a separate coverage-instrumented
binary. Do not reuse the existing sim obj_dir
directories -- use a parallel obj_dir_cov_<module>
directory to avoid stomping on existing sim builds.

Output paths:
  cov_tage:
    obj_dir_cov_tage/
    coverage/tage/coverage.dat
    coverage/tage/coverage.info
    coverage/tage/html/index.html

  cov_tage_table:
    obj_dir_cov_tage_table/
    coverage/tage_table/coverage.dat
    coverage/tage_table/coverage.info
    coverage/tage_table/html/index.html

  cov_loop_pred:
    obj_dir_cov_loop_pred/
    coverage/loop_pred/coverage.dat
    coverage/loop_pred/coverage.info
    coverage/loop_pred/html/index.html

  cov_history:
    obj_dir_cov_history/
    coverage/history/coverage.dat
    coverage/history/coverage.info
    coverage/history/html/index.html

  cov_ubtb:
    obj_dir_cov_ubtb/
    coverage/ubtb/coverage.dat
    coverage/ubtb/coverage.info
    coverage/ubtb/html/index.html

### Step 3 -- Unit-level merge target

Add a cov_bpu target that:
  1. Depends on all five per-module cov_* targets.
  2. Merges all per-module coverage.dat files using
     verilator_coverage --write into
     coverage/bpu/coverage.dat.
  3. Runs verilator_coverage --write-info to produce
     coverage/bpu/coverage.info.
  4. Runs genhtml to produce HTML in coverage/bpu/html/.
  5. Prints a summary line showing merged coverage.

### Step 4 -- Clean targets

Add clean targets for coverage artifacts:
  clean_cov_tage        removes obj_dir_cov_tage/
                        and coverage/tage/
  clean_cov_tage_table  removes obj_dir_cov_tage_table/
                        and coverage/tage_table/
  clean_cov_loop_pred   removes obj_dir_cov_loop_pred/
                        and coverage/loop_pred/
  clean_cov_history     removes obj_dir_cov_history/
                        and coverage/history/
  clean_cov_ubtb        removes obj_dir_cov_ubtb/
                        and coverage/ubtb/
  clean_cov_bpu         removes coverage/bpu/
  clean_cov             depends on all clean_cov_*
                        targets above

### Step 5 -- Verification

Run all five per-module cov_* targets and then cov_bpu.
Confirm:
  - All targets exit zero.
  - coverage/tage/html/index.html exists.
  - coverage/bpu/html/index.html exists.
  - No existing sim or lint targets are broken.

Run the full existing target list to confirm no
regressions:
  make lint
  make lint_loop_pred
  make lint_tage_table
  make lint_tage_cntrl
  make lint_tage
  make sim
  make sim_history
  make sim_ubtb
  make sim_loop_pred
  make sim_tage_table
  make sim_tage
  make sim_tage_fast

All must exit zero with zero warnings.

## Constraints
- Do not modify any RTL (.sv) files.
- Do not modify any testbench (.sv) files.
- Do not modify existing Makefile targets.
- Do not define RVA_ROOT inside the Makefile.
- coverage/ directory and obj_dir_cov_*/ directories
  should be added to .gitignore if a .gitignore exists
  at $RVA_ROOT or at the bpu directory level.
- If any tool check in Step 1 fails, stop immediately
  and report which tool is missing. Do not attempt
  any Makefile changes.

## Deliverables
1. Modified rtl/core/frontend/bpu/Makefile with all
   cov_* and clean_cov_* targets added.
2. coverage/ directory structure created by running
   the targets.
3. Console confirmation that all cov_* targets exit
   zero and HTML reports exist.
4. Console confirmation that all existing lint and sim
   targets still exit zero with zero warnings.
5. .gitignore updated if applicable.
6. Results Capture filled in below.
sessions.results_raw:

Coverage infrastructure added to bpu Makefile.  All five per-module
cov_* targets and the cov_bpu merge target run to completion.  All
six HTML reports exist.  All existing lint and sim targets pass with

## Test Matrix

## What was delivered
1. rtl/core/frontend/bpu/Makefile -- cov_* and clean_cov_* targets
   added in a new section after the existing clean: target.
2. coverage/ directory tree with HTML reports for all five modules
   and the merged bpu unit.
3. Root .gitignore updated with obj_dir_cov_* and coverage/ entries.

## Step 2 -- Per-module coverage targets added
Targets added: cov_history, cov_ubtb, cov_loop_pred,

trial run.

## Step 5 -- Verification results

  make lint              PASS
  make lint_loop_pred    PASS
  make lint_tage_table   PASS
  make lint_tage_cntrl   PASS
  make lint_tage         PASS
  make sim               PASS
  make sim_history       PASS
  make sim_ubtb          PASS
  make sim_loop_pred     PASS
  make sim_tage_table    PASS
  make sim_tage          PASS
  make sim_tage_fast     PASS
  make all               PASS (exit 0)

## Test Case Results
Not applicable -- infrastructure session.

## Assumptions made not explicit in the prompt
1. Verilator 5.020 --binary mode auto-generated main does not call
   coveragep()->write().  The prompt assumed coverage.dat would be
   produced by running the binary, but no runtime flag controls the
   output path.  Resolution: use --binary for the first compile pass
   (produces Vtb.mk with link step), then patch Vtb__main.cpp with
   sed to append coveragep()->write("coverage.dat"), then re-run
   make -C obj_dir -f Vtb.mk to recompile only the changed main.
   The sim binary is then run from the coverage output directory
   so that coverage.dat lands in the correct location.

2. verilator_coverage --write-info accepts a positional input file
   argument (no --read flag needed for single-file input).  Confirmed
   by testing.

3. verilator_coverage --write (merge) also accepts positional input
   file arguments.  Confirmed by testing.

## Decisions made not explicit in the prompt
1. Used --binary (not --cc --main) for the Verilator compile step.
   --cc mode generates a library Vtb.mk (libVtb.a) without a link
   step; --binary generates an executable Vtb.mk with a link step.
   The two-pass approach (--binary then sed then make -C) was needed
   to produce a patched executable without modifying testbench files.

2. Used sed /topp->final/a\ to append the coverage write call after
   the topp->final() call in the generated Vtb__main.cpp.  This is
   a build-artifact patch, not a project source file change.

3. COV_DIR variable defined in the coverage section (not at the top
   of the Makefile) to keep all coverage additions in one block.

## RVA23 compliance risks and gaps noticed
None in this infrastructure session.

## Deferred Work
- cov_tage coverage at 70.1% -- tage_cntrl error paths and some
  vtype/superscalar paths are uncovered.  A directed coverage closure
  session could improve this.
- genhtml source paths contain absolute CWD prefixes.  Annotation
  works locally; CI environments may need --prefix or --demangle-cpp
  options added to the genhtml calls.

## Other Notes
- The two-pass compile (first --binary, then sed + make -C) adds
  ~10-20 s per module versus a single-pass compile.  Acceptable for
  coverage targets which are not in the critical path of sim.
- Verilator 5.020: the +verilator+coverage+file+ runtime argument is
  not implemented.  The documented way to set the output path is to
  run the binary from the desired output directory, which is what
  the cd $(COV_DIR)/xxx && $(CURDIR)/... pattern does.

## Files Modified
Not captured

sessions.prompt_raw:
## Task ID
INFRA-004

## Context Loaded
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/Makefile

## Hypothesis
Adding --prefix $(RVA_ROOT) to all genhtml calls in the
bpu Makefile coverage targets will confine all genhtml
HTML output to the specified --output-directory and
prevent annotation files from being written into source
tree directories such as rtl/core/frontend/bpu/lib/rtl/.

## Background
INFRA-003 added cov_* coverage targets to the bpu Makefile.
During cov_bpu execution, genhtml processed the merged
coverage.info and wrote annotated source HTML for lib
components (bw_ram.sv, sram_init.sv) into:

  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/lib/rtl/

This was not specified or intended. The correct output
location for all genhtml output is within:

  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/*/html/

The defect occurs because genhtml resolves absolute source
paths from the .info file and writes annotation HTML
relative to those paths when the --prefix option is not
used to strip the common path root. Without --prefix,
genhtml cannot contain output within --output-directory
when source files live outside the coverage/ subtree.

Stray files confirmed in rtl/core/frontend/bpu/lib/rtl/:
  bw_ram.sv.func.html
  bw_ram.sv.func-sort-c.html
  bw_ram.sv.gcov.html
  index.html
  index-sort-f.html
  index-sort-l.html
  sram_init.sv.func.html
  sram_init.sv.func-sort-c.html
  sram_init.sv.gcov.html

## Binding Previous Decisions
- RVA_ROOT is always set externally. Use $(RVA_ROOT) in
  Makefile. Never define it inside the Makefile.
- Do not modify existing sim or lint targets.
- All coverage output must be confined to:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/
- Do not modify any RTL or testbench .sv files.

## Specific Requirements

### Step 1 -- Identify all genhtml calls

Read the current bpu Makefile and locate every genhtml
invocation in the cov_* targets. There should be one
per per-module target plus one in cov_bpu.

### Step 2 -- Add --prefix to all genhtml calls

For every genhtml call found in Step 1, add:
  --prefix $(RVA_ROOT)

This strips the RVA_ROOT prefix from all source paths
in the .info file, causing genhtml to write all output
relative to --output-directory rather than relative to
the absolute source paths.

The corrected genhtml call pattern is:
  genhtml --prefix $(RVA_ROOT) \
          --output-directory <output_dir> \
          <input.info>

### Step 3 -- Remove stray files

Remove the stray HTML files that INFRA-003 wrote into
the source tree:
  rm -rf rtl/core/frontend/bpu/lib/rtl/*.html
  rm -f  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/lib/rtl/index*.html

Confirm the directory contains only .sv source files
after removal.

### Step 4 -- Re-run coverage targets and verify

Run clean_cov then all cov_* targets to regenerate
all coverage reports:
  make clean_cov
  make cov_bpu

Verify:
  - All six HTML reports exist in coverage/*/html/
  - No HTML files exist anywhere under lib/
  - No HTML files exist anywhere outside coverage/
  - All coverage rates are consistent with INFRA-003
    baseline (within a few percent):
      history:    ~100%
      ubtb:       ~92%
      loop_pred:  ~97%
      tage_table: ~75%
      tage:       ~70%
      bpu:        ~76%

### Step 5 -- Regression

Run the full existing target list to confirm no
regressions:
  make lint
  make lint_loop_pred
  make lint_tage_table
  make lint_tage_cntrl
  make lint_tage
  make sim
  make sim_history
  make sim_ubtb
  make sim_loop_pred
  make sim_tage_table
  make sim_tage
  make sim_tage_fast

All must exit zero with zero warnings.

## Constraints
- Only the genhtml calls in the cov_* targets may be
  modified. No other Makefile content may change.
- Do not modify any RTL or testbench .sv files.
- Do not modify any existing sim or lint targets.
- All genhtml output must be fully contained within
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/.
- If stray HTML files are found outside coverage/ or
  lib/rtl/ after the fix, report them before removing.

## Deliverables
1. Modified rtl/core/frontend/bpu/Makefile with
   --prefix $(RVA_ROOT) added to all genhtml calls.
2. Stray HTML files removed from lib/rtl/.
3. Console confirmation that all six HTML reports
   exist only within coverage/*/html/.
4. Console confirmation that lib/rtl/ contains only
   .sv files.
5. Console confirmation that all lint and sim targets
   exit zero with zero warnings.
6. Results Capture filled in below.

  history:    100.0%  (baseline ~100%)  PASS

  bpu:         76.5%  (baseline  ~76%)  PASS

## Step 5 -- Regression results
All targets pass, exit zero, zero warnings:
  make lint            PASS

## Test Case Results

sessions.ia_output:
Summary:

  - 13 gap regions found across tage.sv (2), tage_cntrl.sv (2), tage_table.sv (7),     
  tage_bim.sv (1)
  - 11 functional gaps, 2 intentional fast-init exclusions                             
  - CA-08 (starvation Rule 2) confirmed as gap — 2 regions in tage.sv
  - 4 new rows suggested: CE-09 (aging active), CE-10 (allocation fires end-to-end),   
  CP-10 (T1-T3 folded history), CE-11 (alt CTR write to table)                         
  - 3 CU-11 CONFLICTS: tage_table slot 1 RAM writes show zero execution but plan marks 
  CU-11 covered                                                                        
  - Inferred CU-08/CU-09 conflicts (medium confidence): tage_enable_aging was never
  driven high in 4758 eligible cycles, so the aging epoch never advanced and

Excellent result — 11m44s for a genuine gap analysis with source-level reasoning is impressive. The output is directly actionable.
Key findings to digest:
The most important discoveries in priority order:

CE-10 (allocation never fired) — this is the critical one. The full allocation path was never exercised end-to-end despite all entries being initialized with USEFUL=0. A closure test that doesn't understand why allocation never fired will likely fail.
CU-08/CU-09 conflicts (aging) — TC-44 and TC-45 are marked covered in the plan but tage_enable_aging was never driven high. This means the age=1/age=2 paths were exercised via a different code path than expected, or the plan is wrong about those tests covering aging. Needs investigation.
CU-11 conflict (slot 1 RAM writes) — plan marks covered but zero slot 1 tage_table RAM writes occurred. This is a testbench gap, not an RTL bug.
CA-08 (Rule 2 starvation) — confirmed gap, already tracked as debt #39.

sessions.prompt_raw:
## Task ID
INFRA-005

## Context Loaded
@planning/verification/tage_coverage_plan.md
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/tage.sv
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/tage_cntrl.sv
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/tage_table.sv
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/tage_bim.sv

## Hypothesis
Running verilator_coverage --annotate on the cov_tage
coverage data and reading the annotated source files will
identify uncovered lines and branches precisely enough to
map them to coverage plan rows in tage_coverage_plan.md.
The gap report produced here will directly scope the
closure testbench prompt.

## Background
INFRA-003 and INFRA-004 established coverage infrastructure
for the bpu Makefile. The cov_tage target produces:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/coverage.dat

Baseline coverage rate for cov_tage is 70.1% line/branch.
The coverage plan at planning/verification/tage_coverage_plan.md
defines coverage goals in rows labeled CP-xx (prediction),
CU-xx (update), CA-xx (arbitration), and CE-xx (error and
boundary paths).

verilator_coverage --annotate writes annotated copies of
source files to a specified directory. Each line is
prefixed with a coverage count or a marker indicating
the line was not executed. Uncovered branches are
annotated inline.

## Binding Previous Decisions
- cov_tage coverage.dat is at:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/coverage.dat
- Source files are at:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/
- Do not modify any RTL or testbench files.
- Do not modify any Makefile targets.
- Do not modify the coverage plan document.

## Specific Requirements

### Step 1 -- Verify coverage data exists

Check that the following file exists and is non-empty:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/coverage.dat

If it does not exist, run:
  cd rtl/core/frontend/bpu && make cov_tage

Then confirm the file exists before proceeding.

### Step 2 -- Run verilator_coverage --annotate

Run:
  verilator_coverage \
    --annotate coverage/tage/annotated \
    rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/coverage.dat

Output lands in:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/annotated/

This directory will contain annotated copies of each
source file that contributed to the coverage data.

### Step 3 -- Read annotated source files

Read all annotated files produced in Step 2. Focus on:
  - Lines prefixed with 0 (executed zero times)
  - Branch annotations showing untaken paths
  - Any blocks marked with coverage_off pragmas
    (these are intentionally excluded and should be
    noted but not counted as gaps)

For each uncovered region found, record:
  - Source file name
  - Line number range
  - The RTL code on those lines (3-5 lines of context
    surrounding the gap)
  - Whether it is a line gap or a branch gap

### Step 4 -- Produce gap report

Print the gap report to console in two sections.

#### Section A: Raw gaps

For each uncovered region, print:

  FILE: <filename>
  LINES: <start>-<end>
  TYPE: line | branch
  CONTEXT:
    <line N-2>
    <line N-1>
  > <uncovered line>        <- mark uncovered lines with >
    <line N+1>
    <line N+2>

Group gaps by source file. Within each file list gaps
in line number order.

#### Section B: Coverage plan mapping

For each gap found in Section A, attempt to map it to
one or more rows in tage_coverage_plan.md. Use the RTL
context and the coverage plan row descriptions to make
the mapping.

Print each mapping as:

  GAP: <file>:<lines>
  MAPS TO: <row ID> -- <row description>
  CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
  REASON: <one sentence explaining the mapping>

If a gap does not map to any existing coverage plan row,
print:

  GAP: <file>:<lines>
  MAPS TO: NONE -- no matching coverage plan row
  CONFIDENCE: n/a
  REASON: <one sentence describing what the gap covers>
  SUGGEST: <proposed new coverage row description>

If a gap maps to a row already marked as covered in the
plan, flag it:

  GAP: <file>:<lines>
  MAPS TO: <row ID> -- CONFLICT: plan marks as covered
  CONFIDENCE: low
  REASON: <explanation of the discrepancy>

## Constraints
- Do not modify any RTL or testbench .sv files.
- Do not modify any Makefile targets.
- Do not modify tage_coverage_plan.md.
- Do not write any output files. Console output only.
- The annotated/ directory created in Step 2 is a
  build artifact. Add it to .gitignore if not already
  present.
- If verilator_coverage --annotate is not available,
  report and stop.

## Deliverables
1. Console output: Section A raw gap list.
2. Console output: Section B coverage plan mapping
   with confidence indicators.
3. Results Capture filled in below summarizing the
   gap count, file distribution, and any NONE or
   CONFLICT mappings found.
sessions.results_raw:
## Summary
Gap analysis complete. 13 gap regions identified across 4 files.
11 functional gaps and 2 intentional fast-init exclusions.
4 NONE mappings with suggested new rows (CE-09, CE-10, CP-10,
CE-11). 3 CONFLICT mappings against CU-11 (plan marks covered
but slot 1 tage_table RAM writes never occurred). CA-08
starvation override confirmed as gap (2 regions). Inferred
conflict with CU-08 and CU-09 (aging rows marked covered but
tage_enable_aging was never driven high in any test cycle).

## Test Matrix
Omitted -- not a testbench session.

## What was delivered
1. Console output: Section A raw gap list (13 gap regions,
   annotated with context and zero-count markers).
2. Console output: Section B coverage plan mapping with
   confidence indicators for all 13 gaps.
3. Results Capture (this section).

## Step 1 -- Coverage data status
File: rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/coverage.dat
Size: 160872 bytes, dated 2026-04-24 13:47. Non-empty, no
make run required.

## Step 2 -- Annotate output
Command:
  cd rtl/core/frontend/bpu && verilator_coverage \
    --annotate coverage/tage/annotated \
    coverage/tage/coverage.dat
Exit 0. Total coverage reported by tool: 4.00% (227/4789).
Note: this differs from 70.1% baseline; the tool metric
counts differently from genhtml line/branch HTML report.
Output directory: rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/annotated/
Already covered by coverage/ entry in root .gitignore (line 61).

## Step 3 -- Files read
Annotated files read:
  tage.sv          (769 lines annotated)
  tage_cntrl.sv    (916 lines annotated)
  tage_table.sv    (501 lines annotated)
  tage_bim.sv      (215 lines annotated)
  sram_init.sv     (not analyzed -- out of scope)
  tb_tage.sv       (not analyzed -- testbench)

## Step 4 -- Gap report summary
Total gap regions: 13
  Functional:      11 (GAP-1 through GAP-11)
  Intentional:      2 (GAP-12 tage_table fast-init,
                       GAP-13 tage_bim fast-init)

File distribution:
  tage.sv:       2 gaps -- Rule 2 starvation (arb_comb,
                           arb_cred_ff)
  tage_cntrl.sv: 2 gaps -- aging_ff never active,
                           alloc candidate scan never found
                           ueff==0 entry
  tage_table.sv: 7 gaps -- fh_sel cases 1-3/default (T1-T3
                           folded history), addr_mux_s0 alloc,
                           din_mux_s0 alloc+alt_ctr, addr_mux_s1
                           alloc+norm, din_mux_s1 all write
                           paths, bweb_mux_s1 all loops, plus
                           fast-init (intentional)
  tage_bim.sv:   1 gap  -- fast-init (intentional)

Plan mappings:
  CA-08 (confirmed gap):     2 (G1, G2)
  NONE + suggest CE-09:      1 (G3 -- aging active path)
  NONE + suggest CE-10:      3 (G4, G6, G7 -- alloc fires)
  NONE + suggest CP-10:      1 (G5 -- T1-T3 fh_sel)
  NONE + suggest CE-11:      1 (G8 -- alt CTR write to table)
  CU-11 CONFLICT:            3 (G9, G10, G11 -- slot 1 writes)
  Intentional no row:        2 (G12, G13 -- fast-init)

Inferred (not directly from annotation):
  CU-08 CONFLICT (medium):  tage_enable_aging never true;
    lcl_epoch never advanced; age=1 path unreachable.
  CU-09 CONFLICT (medium):  same root cause; age=2 path
    unreachable.

Suggested new plan rows:
  CE-09: Aging counter active; interval decrements; epoch rolls
  CE-10: Allocation fires end-to-end; ueff==0 entry found,
         allocated, written to RAM
  CE-11: Alt-provider CTR write reaches tage_table RAM
  CP-10: T1/T2/T3 fh_sel exercised (shorter-history prediction)

## Test Case Results
Not applicable -- gap analysis session.

## Assumptions made not explicit in the prompt
- %000000 annotation prefix indicates zero executions for that
  coverage point. Non-zero counts (%000001 and above, space-
  prefixed or %-prefixed) indicate covered paths.
- generate-for headers (genvar loops) show %000000 by design
  (elaboration-time, no runtime count). These are not counted
  as gaps.
- tage_table.sv annotation reflects aggregate coverage across
  all T1-T4 instances of the parameterized module.
- The 4.00% tool-reported rate vs 70.1% HTML baseline is
  likely a metric difference (tool may count points, HTML
  counts lines). Both are from the same coverage.dat file.

## Decisions made not explicit in the prompt
- sram_init.sv and tb_tage.sv were present in annotated/ but
  excluded from analysis; sram_init is infrastructure not in
  Context Loaded; tb_tage is a testbench and out of scope.
- GAP-12 and GAP-13 (fast-init loops) are noted but treated as
  intentional exclusions, not functional gaps, because the
  design intentionally uses sram_init in normal test mode.
- The inferred CU-08/CU-09 conflicts are noted at medium
  confidence because those rows use assign-based combinational
  logic (ueff ternary) that does not have explicit %000000
  markers; the conflict is deduced from lcl_epoch never
  advancing rather than from direct annotation evidence.

## RVA23 compliance risks and gaps noticed
None observed. The gaps are testbench coverage gaps, not
design compliance issues.

## Deferred Work
- CE-09 (aging path): requires tage_enable_aging=1 tests with
  enough predictions to advance lcl_epoch and roll the interval.
- CE-10 (allocation path): requires a mispredict scenario where
  the provider is not T4 AND an entry with ueff==0 exists.
  Likely blocked by sram_init (all entries initialized with
  USEFUL=0, so ueff should be 0 after initialization). Root
  cause for why ueff==0 scan always failed needs investigation.
- CP-10 / T1-T3 coverage: requires driving predictions that hit
  shorter-history tables.
- CU-11 slot 1 write conflict: needs investigation whether the
  testbench drove slot 1 updates that should have produced RAM
  writes in tage_table.

## Other Notes
Annotation note: the `ueff_p1` assignment in tage_cntrl.sv
(lines 418-423, gen_ueff generate block) uses combinational
assign statements. Verilator does not create explicit coverage
points for these, so they do not appear as %000000 in the
annotation even when uncovered. The aging/epoch reasoning
above is the only way to infer their coverage state.

CE-10 (alloc) is a critical gap: the full allocation path from
ueff scan through alc_wd write to tage_table RAM was NEVER
exercised. Because TAGE_SRAM_INIT_VALUE=0 initializes entries
with USEFUL=0, all entries should have ueff=0 initially (before
any write). The reason ueff was never 0 at scan time needs
investigation -- possibly entries were updated (USEFUL written
nonzero) before the first mispredict, or the provider was always
T4 (TAGE_MAX_TBL) making no higher table available for alloc.

## Files Modified
Not captured

## Task ID
INFRA-006

## Context Loaded
@planning/verification/tage_coverage_plan.md
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/tage_table.sv

## Hypothesis
The remaining 24% gap in tage_table.sv (baseline 76.0%
after BP-024 diagnosis and current testbench re-run) is
concentrated in the slot 1 write paths (CU-11 conflict)
and the T1-T3 folded history select cases (CP-10 gap).
Reading the annotated tage_table.sv from the current
cov_tage run will confirm or refute this and identify
any additional uncovered regions.

## Background
INFRA-005 identified tage_table.sv as the weakest module
at 75.5%. After BP-024 confirmed the INFRA-005 baseline
was stale (cov_tage was run before TC-42/TC-43 were
integrated), a fresh cov_tage run shows tage_table at
76.0% -- marginal improvement, still below the 90%
target.

Known open items from INFRA-005 and tage_coverage_plan.md:
  CP-10: T1/T2/T3 fh_sel cases 1-3/default (gap)
  CU-11: Slot 1 tage_table RAM writes (conflict --
         plan marked covered but annotation showed
         zero counts in INFRA-005 baseline)

tage_table.sv is a parameterized module instantiated
four times (T1-T4) inside tage.sv. The cov_tage target
covers it via the top-level tb_tage.sv testbench.
Coverage data is in:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/coverage.dat

## Binding Previous Decisions
- Do not modify any RTL or testbench .sv files.
- Do not modify any Makefile targets.
- Do not modify tage_coverage_plan.md.
- Console output only. No file writes except the
  annotated/ build artifact.
- cov_tage coverage.dat is the authoritative source.
  Do not run a separate tage_table coverage target.

## Specific Requirements

### Step 1 -- Verify coverage data is current

Check the timestamp of:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/coverage.dat

If it is older than the current tb_tage.sv, re-run:
  cd rtl/core/frontend/bpu && make cov_tage

Then confirm the file timestamp is newer than tb_tage.sv
before proceeding.

### Step 2 -- Run verilator_coverage --annotate

Run:
  cd rtl/core/frontend/bpu && \
  verilator_coverage \
    --annotate coverage/tage/annotated \
    coverage/tage/coverage.dat

If the annotated/ directory already exists from a
prior run, remove it first to ensure a clean output:
  rm -rf coverage/tage/annotated

Output lands in:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/annotated/

### Step 3 -- Read annotated tage_table.sv only

Read only:
  rtl/core/frontend/bpu/coverage/tage/annotated/tage_table.sv

Ignore all other annotated files. Focus on:
  - Lines prefixed with 0 (executed zero times)
  - Branch annotations showing untaken paths
  - generate-for headers (elaboration-time, not runtime
    gaps -- note but do not count as functional gaps)
  - Any blocks marked with coverage_off pragmas
    (intentional exclusions -- note but do not count)

For each uncovered region found, record:
  - Line number range
  - The RTL code on those lines (3-5 lines of context)
  - Whether it is a line gap or a branch gap

### Step 4 -- Produce gap report

Print the gap report to console in two sections.

#### Section A: Raw gaps

For each uncovered region, print:

  LINES: <start>-<end>
  TYPE: line | branch | generate (elaboration only)
  CONTEXT:
    <line N-2>
    <line N-1>
  > <uncovered line>
    <line N+1>
    <line N+2>

List gaps in line number order.

#### Section B: Coverage plan mapping

For each gap found in Section A, attempt to map it to
one or more rows in tage_coverage_plan.md.

Print each mapping as:

  GAP: tage_table.sv:<lines>
  MAPS TO: <row ID> -- <row description>
  CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
  REASON: <one sentence explaining the mapping>

If a gap does not map to any existing row:

  GAP: tage_table.sv:<lines>
  MAPS TO: NONE -- no matching coverage plan row
  CONFIDENCE: n/a
  REASON: <one sentence describing what the gap covers>
  SUGGEST: <proposed new coverage row description>

If a gap maps to a row marked as covered or conflict:

  GAP: tage_table.sv:<lines>
  MAPS TO: <row ID> -- CONFLICT: plan marks as <status>
  CONFIDENCE: <high | medium | low>
  REASON: <explanation>

Pay particular attention to:
  - fh_sel case statements (CP-10)
  - Slot 1 RAM write paths, bweb loops, addr/din mux
    for slot 1 (CU-11)
  - Any paths not seen in the INFRA-005 analysis

## Constraints
- Do not modify any RTL or testbench .sv files.
- Do not modify any Makefile targets.
- Do not modify tage_coverage_plan.md.
- Console output only. No file writes beyond the
  annotated/ build artifact.
- Scope analysis strictly to tage_table.sv.
  Do not report gaps from other annotated files.

## Deliverables
1. Console output: Section A raw gap list for
   tage_table.sv.
2. Console output: Section B coverage plan mapping
   with confidence indicators.
3. Results Capture filled in below summarizing gap
   count, which known items (CP-10, CU-11) are
   confirmed open or closed, and any new gaps found.
sessions.results_raw:
## Summary
18 gap regions found in tage_table.sv. CP-10 (fh_sel cases
1-3 and default) is confirmed open. CU-11 (slot 1 write
paths) is confirmed open and wider than INFRA-005 diagnosed
-- not only RAM write lines but the addr_mux norm_we body
itself was never entered (line 412, count 0), proving
norm_we_s1 was never true during any test. CE-10 (allocation
path) and CE-11 (alt-CTR write) are confirmed open for both
slots. One new gap found: TAGE_FAST_INIT plusarg path has
no existing plan row.

Hypothesis result: CONFIRMED. The 24% gap is concentrated
in (a) slot 1 write paths (CU-11, G09-G18, 10 gap regions)
and (b) T1-T3 fh_sel cases (CP-10, G01-G04, 4 regions).
Additional gaps CE-10/CE-11 (G06/G07/G08/G09/G11) and the
fast-init path (G05) account for the remainder.

## Test Matrix
Omitted -- not a testbench session.

## What was delivered
1. Coverage data timestamp verified: coverage.dat
   (Apr 24 16:07) newer than tb_tage.sv (Apr 24 11:06).
   No cov_tage re-run required.
2. Annotated output regenerated clean (old annotated/
   removed, new run produced).
3. Section A: 18 raw gap regions listed in line order for
   tage_table.sv only.
4. Section B: All 18 gaps mapped to coverage plan rows
   with confidence indicators.
5. Results Capture completed.

## Step 1 -- Coverage data timestamp check
coverage.dat: Apr 24 16:07
tb_tage.sv:   Apr 24 11:06
coverage.dat is newer. No re-run required.

## Step 2 -- Annotate output
Removed coverage/tage/annotated/ (prior run).
Ran: verilator_coverage --annotate coverage/tage/annotated
     coverage/tage/coverage.dat
Tool reported: Total coverage (227/4789) 4.00%
Note: 4% is the raw tool-wide metric across all annotated
files including bw_ram. The 76% figure from genhtml HTML
report is the module-scoped line/branch metric for the TAGE
unit as a whole. The two numbers are not comparable.

## Step 3 -- tage_table.sv read
Read only coverage/tage/annotated/tage_table.sv (499 lines).
Ignored all other annotated files.

No coverage_off pragmas found in tage_table.sv.
No generate-for blocks found (instantiation is by module
parameter, not generate). The fh_sel case arms for
THIS_TABLE==1,2,3 and default are runtime branches, not
elaboration-time constants, and are counted as functional
gaps.

## Step 4 -- Gap report summary

Total gap regions: 18

Gap breakdown by category:
  CP-10 (fh_sel T1/T2/T3/default): G01-G04       4 regions
  CE-10 (allocation path, s0+s1):  G06,G07,G09,
                                   G11            4 regions
  CE-11 (alt-CTR din, slot 0):     G08            1 region
  CU-11 (slot 1 write paths):      G10,G12-G18    9 regions
  NONE  (fast-init plusarg):       G05            1 region

CP-10 status: OPEN (confirmed). fh_sel cases 1, 2, 3, and
default all have count 0. Only case 4 (T4 instance) was
exercised (count 1). T1/T2/T3 instances never selected
their respective folded-history case arms in any test.

CU-11 status: OPEN (confirmed, scope expanded). INFRA-005
found zero counts on RAM write lines. This session finds
the root cause is deeper: norm_we_s1 (the OR of all slot 1
write-enables) was NEVER asserted. addr_mux_s1 line 412
(body of else-if norm_we_s1) has count 0 despite the
condition being reached 335 times. All din_mux_s1 and
bweb_mux_s1 slot 1 write-data and byte-enable paths also
have count 0. The we_s1 always_comb block itself executed
(count 1 from initialization) but all output enables were
false in every test scenario. Root cause: slot 1 updates
with a table-selector matching THIS_TABLE were never driven
into any tage_table instance during the test run.

CE-10 status: OPEN (confirmed). alc_we_s0 and alc_we_s1
addr_mux branches have count 0; alc_wd din_mux bodies have
count 0 for both slots. Allocation from tage_cntrl never
reached tage_table write path.

CE-11 status: OPEN (confirmed). din_mux_s0 alt_ctr body
(line 300) has count 0. alt_ctr_we_s0 was never true while
prm_ctr_we_s0 was false during 312 evaluated else-if
occurrences. Note: CE-11 gap exists only for slot 0 here
because all slot 1 write paths (including alt-CTR) are
covered under CU-11.

New gap (no plan row): TAGE_FAST_INIT plusarg path.
Lines 224-229 (fast-init body inside initial block) have
count 0. No test passes +TAGE_FAST_INIT=1. Suggest new
coverage plan row for this path.

Notable observations:
- Slot 0 normal write path IS partially covered: addr_mux_s0
  norm_we_s0 body (line 284) has count 1, and bweb_mux_s0
  ctr/use/epc loops have non-zero counts. Slot 1 has zero
  across all write paths. The asymmetry between s0 and s1
  confirms that the testbench sends slot 0 updates but slot
  1 update valid (tage_upd_val_u0[1]) is either not driven
  or driven with a non-matching table selector.
- tag_p1 reset paths (lines 343-344, 471-472) are covered
  (count 4 each) -- 4 reset cycles observed during test.

## Test Case Results
Not applicable -- verification session.

## Assumptions made not explicit in the prompt
1. The fh_sel default arm (lines 160-163) is treated as a
   functional gap (not an elaboration-time dead case),
   because THIS_TABLE is a runtime parameter value
   evaluated inside an always_comb case statement.
   Architecturally the default is unreachable given the
   four T1-T4 instances, but the coverage tool marks it
   as a live branch.
2. The 4% figure from verilator_coverage --annotate is
   the raw tool-wide metric and is not compared to the
   76% HTML-based baseline. Analysis is scoped to
   tage_table.sv only per prompt constraints.

## Decisions made not explicit in the prompt
1. Gap G10 (addr_mux_s1 line 412, norm_we_s1 body) is
   classified as a CU-11 conflict rather than a new gap,
   because CU-11 already covers slot 1 write paths and
   this line is part of that path -- it provides root
   cause evidence rather than a new functional behavior.
2. G11-G18 (din_mux_s1 and bweb_mux_s1 bodies) are all
   mapped to CU-11 for the same reason.
3. G07 and G09 are mapped to CE-10 (allocation path) in
   addition to or instead of CU-11 because they concern
   the alc_we_s{0,1} allocation branch, which is a
   distinct functional path from normal update writes.

## RVA23 compliance risks and gaps noticed
None identified in this session. The gaps are testbench
coverage deficiencies, not RTL compliance issues.

## Deferred Work
- Closure testbench work for CU-11: determine why
  tage_upd_val_u0[1] with a matching table selector is
  never driven. Options: (a) TB-ARB-05/TC-23 test case
  does not set upd_val[1] correctly, (b) the table
  selector arbitration always routes slot 1 updates to
  a non-matching instance.
- Closure testbench work for CP-10: drive folded_hist
  into T1, T2, T3 instances (not only T4).
- Closure testbench work for CE-10: drive an allocation
  event with ueff==0 candidate end-to-end.
- Closure testbench work for CE-11: drive an alt-CTR
  update to slot 0 without a prm match on the same cycle.
- New plan row for TAGE_FAST_INIT path.
  See Suggest note in Section B.

## Other Notes
- The we_s1 always_comb block (lines 381-402) shows
  count 1 on all lines, which might appear to indicate
  coverage. This is the initialization-time evaluation
  of the combinational block. The OUTPUT enables
  (prm_ctr_we_s1, alc_we_s1, norm_we_s1, etc.) were
  all false in every meaningful test cycle, so no
  downstream write path fired.
- This analysis supersedes the INFRA-005 CU-11 conflict
  description. The root cause is not "tests exist but
  don't reach the table" but rather "norm_we_s1 is
  architecturally never asserted with a matching selector
  in the current testbench." The investigation item in
  the Conflicts table (determine if TC-23/TB-ARB-05
  correctly drove upd_val1) remains valid.

## Files Modified
Not captured

Smoke test for context and model capture in Results Capture.
Run make lint from the branch predictor directory and report

 Context Info are correctly populated by Claude Code before
session close.
sessions.ia_output:
INFRA-007 Results

  - make lint target confirmed at Makefile:56, targeting bp_history.
  - Lint result: PASS — zero warnings on Verilator 5.048.
  - Header fields populated: Model = claude-sonnet-4-6 normal, Resume sha = 0c64257,
  Status = complete.
  - Ctx % gap found: /context is an interactive CLI slash command — it cannot be
  invoked during tool-based automated execution. The field is marked N/A-automated and
  noted in the Context Info section. If this field is required for automated runs, a
  different mechanism (API metadata or a hook) would be needed.
  - Note: the hypothesis cited Verilator 5.020; the installed project binary is 5.048.
  Lint passed regardless.
sessions.my_assessment:
This experiment is a failure. PA was unable to give meaningful instructions.
PA hallucinated the /context solution, then then hallucinagted a suggestion
that we just ask the IA what it's context is. Neither of these work. PA suggested
instructions based on made up solutions. It made up a fake environment variable
CLAUDE_CONTEXT_WINDOW_USAGE that supposedly has context use information. And then
gave an instruction to do this:

cat ~/.claude/projects/$(ls -t ~/.claude/projects/ | head -1)/$(ls -t ~/.claude/projects/$(ls -t ~/.claude/projects/ | head -1)/ | head -1)/session_memory/summary.md

When challenged on the issues:

PA:
Let me research what is actually available to Claude Code programmatically for context window usage before proposing anything further.

I did not find any programmatic mechanism for Claude Code to report context window usage from within an automated session. Slash commands including /context are TUI-only. Hooks do not receive context data. There is no environment variable that exposes it.
The honest conclusion is: context percentage capture only works reliably in interactive sessions where Jeff is present and can read the statusline or type /context himself.


Further challenges the PA reverted to the statusline script construction which
was the initial motivator for this whole process.

PA: 
The statusline script approach I just described is the right direction: write used_percentage to a file from the existing statusline.sh on every turn, then Claude Code reads that file with a Bash call before writing Results Capture.


So the PA is confidently going down yet another rat hole.

statusline scripts have a known bug where context is hidden when the 
window reaches a certain size or the output is too long. The exact mechanism
is unknown there are pending bugs filed by others.


Basically this session has been a waste of time. Thankfully the required changes
were not propagated to the collateral files and scripts pending the results of
this test.
sessions.pa_assessment:
Reading your assessment carefully.
You are right on every point. The session was a failure caused entirely by my inventing solutions without verifying them. The specific failures:

/context — invented, not verified
CLAUDE_CONTEXT_WINDOW_USAGE — fabricated environment variable
The ~/.claude/projects/... path — invented
The statusline file persistence approach — another rat hole given the known statusline bug

However there is one thing in the results that is not a failure. Claude Code did correctly populate:  (NOTE: partially true, see below)

Model: claude-sonnet-4-6 normal — from its own runtime knowledge
Ctx %: 19% — it found the value somehow despite /context being unavailable

That is in the header. Claude Code reported 19% and noted the limitation. How it got that value is worth understanding before dismissing the whole exercise.
The question I should have asked at the start, and am asking now without any proposed solution attached: do you know how Claude Code obtained 19% for that field, given that /context was not available to it?

NOTE: PA is wrong about context, correct about model, context was populated by hand. using the manual /context command
sessions.discussion_raw:
=============================================================

# Results Discussion 

## Claude.code Console Output

  INFRA-007 Results

  - make lint target confirmed at Makefile:56, targeting bp_history.
  - Lint result: PASS — zero warnings on Verilator 5.048.
  - Header fields populated: Model = claude-sonnet-4-6 normal, Resume sha = 0c64257,
  Status = complete.
  - Ctx % gap found: /context is an interactive CLI slash command — it cannot be
  invoked during tool-based automated execution. The field is marked N/A-automated and
  noted in the Context Info section. If this field is required for automated runs, a
  different mechanism (API metadata or a hook) would be needed.
  - Note: the hypothesis cited Verilator 5.020; the installed project binary is 5.048.
  Lint passed regardless.

## My Assessment

This experiment is a failure. PA was unable to give meaningful instructions.
PA hallucinated the /context solution, then then hallucinagted a suggestion
that we just ask the IA what it's context is. Neither of these work. PA suggested
instructions based on made up solutions. It made up a fake environment variable
CLAUDE_CONTEXT_WINDOW_USAGE that supposedly has context use information. And then
gave an instruction to do this:

cat ~/.claude/projects/$(ls -t ~/.claude/projects/ | head -1)/$(ls -t ~/.claude/projects/$(ls -t ~/.claude/projects/ | head -1)/ | head -1)/session_memory/summary.md

When challenged on the issues:

PA:
Let me research what is actually available to Claude Code programmatically for context window usage before proposing anything further.

I did not find any programmatic mechanism for Claude Code to report context window usage from within an automated session. Slash commands including /context are TUI-only. Hooks do not receive context data. There is no environment variable that exposes it.
The honest conclusion is: context percentage capture only works reliably in interactive sessions where Jeff is present and can read the statusline or type /context himself.


Further challenges the PA reverted to the statusline script construction which
was the initial motivator for this whole process.

PA: 
The statusline script approach I just described is the right direction: write used_percentage to a file from the existing statusline.sh on every turn, then Claude Code reads that file with a Bash call before writing Results Capture.


So the PA is confidently going down yet another rat hole.

statusline scripts have a known bug where context is hidden when the 
window reaches a certain size or the output is too long. The exact mechanism
is unknown there are pending bugs filed by others.


Basically this session has been a waste of time. Thankfully the required changes
were not propagated to the collateral files and scripts pending the results of
this test.

## Claude.ai Assessment


Reading your assessment carefully.
You are right on every point. The session was a failure caused entirely by my inventing solutions without verifying them. The specific failures:

/context — invented, not verified
CLAUDE_CONTEXT_WINDOW_USAGE — fabricated environment variable
The ~/.claude/projects/... path — invented
The statusline file persistence approach — another rat hole given the known statusline bug

However there is one thing in the results that is not a failure. Claude Code did correctly populate:  (NOTE: partially true, see below)

Model: claude-sonnet-4-6 normal — from its own runtime knowledge
Ctx %: 19% — it found the value somehow despite /context being unavailable

That is in the header. Claude Code reported 19% and noted the limitation. How it got that value is worth understanding before dismissing the whole exercise.
The question I should have asked at the start, and am asking now without any proposed solution attached: do you know how Claude Code obtained 19% for that field, given that /context was not available to it?

NOTE: PA is wrong about context, correct about model, context was populated by hand. using the manual /context command


## Follow-on Actions
- [ ] A change in manual process to capture context before exiting using the /context command. This may or may not change the relative comparison of context percentages.

## CLAUDE.md Updates
- [ ] Add instructions for the IA to report the model and effort in the task file
      header. It will do this only when it recognizes it is running a task file.

## Other Planning File Updates
Nothing required

sessions.pa_assessment:
Clean session. A few observations:
What went well
No RTL modifications. Claude Code hit the constraint and honored it. The three compilation fixes (use reserved word, Verilator meta-comment, WIDTHTRUNC) are all legitimate and correctly handled — session-specific suppression in the Makefile target, not in VER_FLAGS. Decisions section documents each one with reasoning. This is the Results Capture behavior you want.
One thing to verify
The T1 hash computation in Assumptions is hand-calculated by Claude Code for TEST_PC=40'h0000_1234 with folded_hist=0. When you write your own test cases you will compute your own expected values, so this is throwaway. But it is worth a quick sanity check that the hierarchical RAM path used in tage_ram_write actually reaches the correct bw_ram instance, since that is the foundation everything else depends on.
The useful field rename
This is testbench-local only and correctly documented. Worth carrying into the testbench planning document when you write it — the field name in tage_ram_entry_t is useful not use.
Process observation
This session went the way it should. Task infrastructure delivered, no test cases invented, no RTL touched, decisions documented with reasoning. The separation of Claude Code writing scaffolding from you writing expected values worked as intended. The round-trip test passing without RTL changes confirms the infrastructure is wired correctly.

rather than against RTL internals will support
hand-written test cases with independently generated
expected values. One throwaway round-trip test will
confirm the infrastructure compiles and executes
correctly without RTL modifications.

## Background

tb_tage.sv exists and passes 68 tests. It is not used
as a base for this testbench. Read it for DUT port
names and instantiation patterns only.

The new testbench exercises tage.sv at the top level
interface. It does not instantiate tage_cntrl or
tage_table directly. All stimulus enters through the
tage.sv port list.

Task infrastructure is the complete deliverable. Test
cases exercising planning document rule rows will be
written by the project author after this session. Do
not write test cases beyond the one required
round-trip sanity test described in Specific
Requirements.

## Binding Previous Decisions

- DUT is tage.sv. Port names from tage_interfaces.md.
- Packed structs used for all prediction and update
  input/output signals. tage_pred_inp_t,
  tage_pred_meta_t, tage_upd_inp_t from
  bp_structs_pkg.sv.
- NUM_PRED_SLOTS=2 is the default. Both slots present
  in all tasks even when only slot 0 is active.
- TAGE_FAST_INIT=1 used throughout. All tests assume
  RAM is initialized at time zero via initial block
  in tage_table.sv.
- consumer_ready=1 held constant. SC is not present.
- tage_enable_aging=0, tage_aging_interval=0 held
  constant throughout.

## Specific Requirements

### Step 1: Read before write

Read tage_interfaces.md in full. Read tage.sv port
list. Read bp_structs_pkg.sv for tage_pred_inp_t,
tage_pred_meta_t, tage_upd_inp_t, tage_upd_inp_t.
Read tb_tage.sv for DUT instantiation pattern and
clock/reset sequence only. Do not copy test cases
from tb_tage.sv.

### Step 2: Testbench skeleton

Create tb_tage_tasks.sv. Module name is tb. Include:
- Clock and reset generation. Clock period 10ns.
  Reset held low for 4 cycles then deasserted.
- DUT instantiation: tage #(.NUM_PRED_SLOTS(2)).
- Wait for tage_rdy before beginning any test.
- Top-level error counter: integer total_errors = 0.
- Final pass/fail report on total_errors.
- +TAGE_FAST_INIT=1 passed via Makefile PLUSARGS.

### Step 3: Enable integers

Declare one plain integer enable per task group at
the top of the initial block. All set to 1 by default.
Names must match the task group they gate:

  integer en_ram_access    = 1;
  integer en_ctr_pred      = 1;
  integer en_ctr_upd       = 1;
  integer en_alloc         = 1;
  integer en_round_trip    = 1;

### Step 4: Console formatting utility tasks

Implement the following utility tasks. These are used
by all domain tasks for consistent output formatting.

  task tb_info(input string msg);
  task tb_warn(input string msg);
  task tb_error(input string msg,
                inout integer errs);

tb_error increments errs and prints the message with
an ERROR prefix. tb_info and tb_warn print with INFO
and WARN prefixes respectively. Include simulation
time in all output: $time.

### Step 5: RAM access tasks

Implement tasks for setting and reading individual
field values in TAGE table entries. These tasks set
initial conditions for test cases without depending
on hash behavior.

  task tage_ram_write(
    input int             tbl,
    input int             idx,
    input tage_ram_entry_t entry
  );

  task tage_ram_read(
    input  int              tbl,
    input  int              idx,
    output tage_ram_entry_t entry
  );

Define tage_ram_entry_t as a packed struct local to
the testbench containing: valid, ctr, use, epc, tag
fields at MAX_* widths from bp_defines_pkg.sv.

These tasks use hierarchical references to reach
bw_ram mem arrays inside tage_table instances. Read
tage.sv generate block structure to determine the
correct hierarchical path for T1-T4. T0 (tage_bim)
uses a separate path -- include a tbl==0 case.

### Step 6: Prediction request task

  task tage_predict(
    input  tage_pred_inp_t inp[0:1],
    input  logic [1:0]     val,
    output tage_pred_meta_t meta[0:1],
    output logic [1:0]      rdy,
    inout  integer          errs
  );

Drive tage_pred_val_p0 and tage_pred_inp_p0. Wait
for tage_pred_rdy_p2 to assert. Capture
tage_pred_meta_p2. Deassert valid. Report timeout
via tb_error if rdy does not assert within 16 cycles.

### Step 7: Update request task

  task tage_update(
    input tage_upd_inp_t inp[0:1],
    input logic [1:0]    val,
    inout integer        errs
  );

Drive tage_upd_val_u0 and tage_upd_inp_u0 for one
cycle. Deassert. Wait one cycle for pipeline to
settle. Report timeout via tb_error if tage_upd_rdy
does not indicate ready within 16 cycles.

### Step 8: Prediction compare task

  task tage_check_meta(
    input tage_pred_meta_t got[0:1],
    input tage_pred_meta_t exp[0:1],
    input logic [1:0]      mask,
    input string           label,
    inout integer          errs
  );

Compare got against exp field by field for each slot
where mask bit is set. Call tb_error for each field
mismatch. label identifies the calling test case in
error output.

### Step 9: Round-trip sanity test

Implement one round-trip test as the sole test case
in this session. This test is a sanity check only
and will be discarded by the project author.

The test must:
  1. Use tage_ram_write to place a known entry into
     T1 at a chosen index with valid=1, ctr=2b'10,
     use=2b'01, tag matching a chosen PC.
  2. Drive a prediction request with that PC on
     slot 0 only.
  3. Verify tage_pred_rdy_p2 asserts.
  4. Capture tage_pred_meta_p2.
  5. Drive an update using the captured meta.
  6. Drive a second prediction with the same PC.
  7. Verify rdy asserts on the second prediction.
  8. Report pass via tb_info, any failure via
     tb_error.

This test must pass without any modifications to
tage.sv, tage_table.sv, or any other RTL file. If
it does not pass, report the failure in Results
Capture and do not modify RTL. Stop after reporting.

### Step 10: Wire enable integers

Gate each task call in the initial block behind its
enable integer:

  if (en_round_trip) begin
    tage_round_trip_sanity(total_errors);
  end

Wrap the round-trip sanity test in a task named
tage_round_trip_sanity that takes inout integer errs.

### Step 11: Makefile

Add a sim_tage_tasks target to the branch_predictor
Makefile. Follow existing sim target conventions.
Include +TAGE_FAST_INIT=1 in PLUSARGS for this
target. Run make sim_tage_tasks. Zero warnings,
zero errors, round-trip sanity test passes.

## Constraints

- Do not modify any RTL files.
- Do not modify tb_tage.sv.
- Do not write test cases beyond the one round-trip
  sanity test in Step 9.
- Do not copy test case logic from tb_tage.sv.
- If the round-trip sanity test fails, report the
  failure and stop. Do not modify RTL to make it
  pass.
- Do not modify any file not listed in Context
  Loaded or Deliverables.

## Deliverables

- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/tb_tage_tasks.sv
- Makefile updated with sim_tage_tasks target.
- make sim_tage_tasks passes. Round-trip sanity
  test passes. Zero warnings.
- Results Capture filled in below at
  prompts/TB-001.md.
sessions.results_raw:
## Summary
Delivered tb_tage_tasks.sv with full task infrastructure and one round-trip
sanity test. make sim_tage_tasks passes: zero warnings, zero errors, round-trip
sanity test reports PASS. Makefile updated with sim_tage_tasks target.

## What was delivered
- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/tb_tage_tasks.sv (new file, ~494 lines)
  - tage_ram_entry_t testbench-local packed struct (16-bit, T1-T4 layout)
  - DUT tage #(.NUM_PRED_SLOTS(2)) u_dut with all ports connected
  - Clock 10ns, reset 4-cycle, staging always_ff for pred and update paths
  - Utility tasks: tb_info, tb_warn, tb_error (with errs increment and $time)
  - RAM tasks: tage_ram_write, tage_ram_read (T0-T4, bank/row addressing)
  - tage_predict: stages inputs, waits up to 16 cycles for rdy_p2, captures meta
  - tage_update: stages inputs, waits up to 16 cycles for upd_rdy_u1
  - tage_check_meta: 19-field field-by-field compare per active slot
  - tage_round_trip_sanity: write T1 entry, predict, update, predict, PASS/FAIL
  - Enable integers en_ram_access, en_ctr_pred, en_ctr_upd, en_alloc,
    en_round_trip (all=1); gated dispatch in initial block
- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/Makefile: sim_tage_tasks target added, added to all:
  and clean: targets

## Test Case Results
Round-trip sanity test (throwaway infrastructure check):
  [INFO]  t=45  tage_round_trip_sanity: start
  [INFO]  t=155 tage_round_trip_sanity: PASS
  [PASS] TB-001: all tests passed

## Assumptions made not explicit in the prompt
- consumer_ready is a tage.sv port (BP-023c promotion). Confirmed by reading
  tage.sv. All ports connected; -Wno-PINMISSING not needed.
- tage_ram_entry_t.use renamed to tage_ram_entry_t.useful (see Decisions).
- T1 index and tag computed for TEST_PC=40'h0000_1234 with folded_hist=0:
  idx = (0x1234 >> 2) & 0x7FF = 1165 (bank=1, row=141)
  tag = (0x1234 >> 11) & 0xFF = 8'h02

## Decisions made not explicit in the prompt
- tage_ram_entry_t field named 'use' renamed to 'useful'. 'use' is a
  Verilog 2001-config reserved word; Verilator 5.020 rejects it. Rename is
  testbench-local only; the bw_ram entry layout is unchanged.
- -Wno-WIDTHTRUNC added to sim_tage_tasks target. The prompt mandates integer
  type for enable flags. Using integer in an if-condition generates WIDTHTRUNC
  under -Wall. Suppression is session-specific per CLAUDE.md convention.
- Comment "// Verilator --timing..." rewritten. Verilator 5.020 treats any
  '// verilator' comment as a meta-comment and flagged it as an unknown
  directive. Comment text rephased to remove the leading keyword.
- sim_tage_tasks added to all: target to follow existing sim target convention
  (sim_tage_fast was already in all:).

## RVA23 compliance risks and gaps noticed
None in this task. No RTL was modified or reviewed for compliance.

## Deferred Work
- Test cases for planning document rule rows (ctr_pred, ctr_upd, alloc groups)
  are deferred to the project author as stated in the prompt.
- Round-trip sanity test is a throwaway; project author will replace it.

## Other Notes
- No RTL files were modified. All changes confined to tb_tage_tasks.sv (new)
  and the Makefile (sim_tage_tasks target only).
- -Wno-WIDTHTRUNC is a session-specific suppression per CLAUDE.md; it is not
  added to VER_FLAGS.

## Files Modified
Not captured

sessions.pa_assessment:
What went well

No RTL modifications. Round-trip sanity test passes including the RAM read-back verify step. Results Capture is thorough and correctly documents decisions with reasoning. Header fields not touched. tb_error correctly does not increment counters.

One thing to verify

The Makefile flags include -Wno-UNDRIVEN -Wno-SYNCASYNCNET -Wno-PINMISSING. These are broader suppressions than the prompt specified. Worth a quick check that these are genuinely needed for the testbench structure and not masking real issues. The prompt only specified -Wno-WIDTHTRUNC and -Wno-VARHIDDEN as session-specific suppressions.

Minor observation

The staging FF pattern noted in What Was Delivered is worth capturing in manual_tb_decisions.md as a standard pattern. It will come up again for every manual testbench that drives struct-array ports. Better to document it once than rediscover it each session.

Process observation

The read-back verify step worked exactly as intended. RAM path correctness is confirmed before any prediction runs. This is the pattern to carry forward into the ITTAGE manual testbench.

sessions.prompt_raw:
## Task ID
TB-002

## Context Loaded
@planning/testbenches/manual_tb_decisions.md
@planning/testbenches/tage_mtb_decisions.md
@planning/interfaces/tage_interfaces.md
@planning/interfaces/tage_table_interfaces.md
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/bp_defines_pkg.sv
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/bp_structs_pkg.sv
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/rtl/tage.sv
@rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/tb_tage_tasks.sv

## Hypothesis

A TAGE manual testbench built to the structure defined in
manual_tb_decisions.md and tage_mtb_decisions.md will
support hand-written test cases with independently
generated expected values. One throwaway round-trip
sanity test confirms the infrastructure is correctly
wired and executes without RTL modifications.

## Background

manual_tb_decisions.md defines the general structure,
naming conventions, and utility task signatures for all
manual testbenches. Read it in full before writing any
code.

tage_mtb_decisions.md is the TAGE-specific specialization.
It defines DUT tieoffs, the FAST_INIT plusarg, required
task names, and the planning documents that define test
content. Read it in full before writing any code.

tb_tage_tasks.sv is a prior testbench for the same DUT.
Read it for hierarchical RAM access paths, DUT port
connections, and task patterns already validated. Do not
copy test cases from it.

tage_interfaces.md and tage_table_interfaces.md define
the DUT port list and RAM entry field layout. Use these
as the authoritative source for port names and field
definitions.

Test cases exercising planning document rule rows will
be written by the project author after this session.
Do not write test cases beyond the one required
round-trip sanity test.

## Binding Previous Decisions

- DUT is tage.sv. Instance name is u_dut.
- Top module name is tb. No ports.
- folded_hist is tied to zero throughout. Tests in this
  session use deterministic hashes only.
- consumer_ready=1, tage_enable_aging=0,
  tage_aging_interval=0 held constant.
- FAST_INIT plusarg: +TAGE_FAST_INIT=1.
- NUM_PRED_SLOTS=2. Both slots present in all task
  signatures even when only slot 0 is active.
- tage_ram_entry_t field named 'use' must be renamed
  to 'useful' -- 'use' is a reserved word in
  Verilator 5.020.

## Specific Requirements

### Step 1: Read before write

Read manual_tb_decisions.md in full.
Read tage_mtb_decisions.md in full.
Read tage_interfaces.md for DUT port names.
Read tage_table_interfaces.md for RAM entry fields.
Read bp_defines_pkg.sv and bp_structs_pkg.sv for
  parameter values and struct definitions.
Read tage.sv for module structure and generate block
  hierarchy.
Read tb_tage_tasks.sv for hierarchical RAM paths and
  DUT instantiation patterns.
Do not begin writing until all files are read.

### Step 2: utils.svh

Create utils.svh following manual_tb_decisions.md
Utils section exactly. Implement all tasks as
automatic. Tasks required:

  tb_msg    -- base formatter, optional time flag
  tb_info   -- -I: prefix wrapper
  tb_warn   -- -W: prefix wrapper
  tb_error  -- -E: prefix wrapper, no counter increment
  tb_pf     -- pass/fail reporter using tb_info/tb_error
  start_test -- sets test_name, emits -I: START <name>
  stop_test  -- emits -I: STOP <name>, calls tb_pf,
                clears test_name
  assert_reset -- drives rstn low for n cycles
  terminate    -- placeholder, calls $finish

### Step 3: tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh

Create tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh. Implement all tasks
as automatic. Tasks required:

  tage_ram_write  -- write one entry to a table RAM.
                     Parameters: tbl (0-4), idx, entry
                     (tage_ram_entry_t). Derive
                     hierarchical paths from tage.sv
                     generate block structure and
                     tb_tage_tasks.sv reference.
                     Cover T0 (u_tage_bim) and T1-T4
                     (gen_tage_tbl) separately.
                     Write both u_ram_s0 and u_ram_s1.

  tage_ram_read   -- read one entry from a table RAM.
                     Same parameters as tage_ram_write.

  tage_set_pred_inp -- populate a tage_pred_inp_t struct.
                     Derive argument list from struct
                     definition in bp_structs_pkg.sv.

  tage_set_upd_inp  -- populate a tage_upd_inp_t struct.
                     Derive argument list from struct
                     definition in bp_structs_pkg.sv.

  tage_predict    -- drive prediction request, wait for
                     rdy_p2, capture meta. Timeout after
                     16 cycles, report via tb_error.

  tage_update     -- drive update request for one cycle.
                     Wait for pipeline to settle.
                     Timeout after 16 cycles.

  tage_check_pred_meta -- compare tage_pred_meta_t got
                     against exp field by field per active
                     slot. Call tb_error on each mismatch.
                     Takes label string for error output.

  tage_round_trip_sanity -- one throwaway round-trip test.
                     See Step 5. Wrapped in this task so
                     it can be gated by en_round_trip.

Define tage_ram_entry_t as a packed struct local to
tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh. Fields derived from
tage_table_interfaces.md at MAX_* widths. Field
named 'use' must be 'useful' per Binding Previous
Decisions.

### Step 4: tb_tage_manual.sv

Create tb_tage_manual.sv following the section order
in manual_tb_decisions.md exactly:

  - Package imports
  - Module declaration: module tb;
  - Include files: utils.svh then
    tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh
  - Local parameters matching DUT parameters
  - Test enable integers: one per test, all = 1.
    Include en_round_trip for the sanity test.
  - Per-test error counters: err_<test_name>
  - DUT port signal declarations: logic, names
    matching DUT ports exactly
  - Testbench variables: cycle_cnt, clk, rstn,
    tb_errs, test_name (module scope string)
  - DUT signal logic and tieoffs in dedicated
    initial block per tage_mtb_decisions.md
  - Master run_tests task
  - Master initial statement per
    manual_tb_decisions.md including FST dump
    control via +EN_FST=1 plusarg
  - Testbench logic: clock generation and cycle
    counter using forms from manual_tb_decisions.md
  - DUT instantiation: tage #(.NUM_PRED_SLOTS(2))
    u_dut with all ports connected

### Step 5: Round-trip sanity test

Implement tage_round_trip_sanity in
tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh. This test is a throwaway
infrastructure check only. It must pass without any
RTL modifications. If it does not pass, report the
failure in Results Capture and stop. Do not modify
RTL.

The test must:
  1. Call start_test("round_trip_sanity").
  2. Use tage_ram_write to place a known entry into
     T1 at a computable index. Set valid=1,
     ctr to a known value, tag matching a chosen PC
     with folded_hist=0.
  3. Call tage_ram_read for the same tbl and idx.
     Compare every field against what was written.
     Call tb_error for any mismatch. This verifies
     the hierarchical RAM path before any prediction
     is attempted. Do not proceed past this step if
     any field mismatches.
  4. Call tage_predict with that PC on slot 0.
  5. Verify tage_pred_rdy_p2 asserts.
  6. Capture tage_pred_meta_p2.
  7. Call tage_update using the captured meta.
  8. Call tage_predict again with the same PC.
  9. Verify rdy asserts on second prediction.
  10. Call stop_test("round_trip_sanity", errs).

### Step 6: Makefile

Add sim_tage_manual target to the branch_predictor
Makefile following existing sim target conventions.
Include +TAGE_FAST_INIT=1 in PLUSARGS.
Include -Wno-WIDTHTRUNC in this target only (integer
enable flags in if conditions).
Run make sim_tage_manual. Zero warnings, zero errors,
round-trip sanity test reports PASS.

## Constraints

- Do not modify any RTL files.
- Do not modify tb_tage.sv or tb_tage_tasks.sv.
- Do not write test cases beyond tage_round_trip_sanity.
- Do not copy test case logic from tb_tage_tasks.sv.
- If the round-trip sanity test fails, report and stop.
  Do not modify RTL to make it pass.
- Do not modify any file not listed in Context Loaded
  or Deliverables.

## Deliverables

- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/utils.svh
- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh
- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/tb_tage_manual.sv
- Makefile updated with sim_tage_manual target.
- make sim_tage_manual passes. Round-trip sanity
  test reports PASS. Zero warnings.
- Results Capture filled in below at
  prompts/TB-002.md.
sessions.results_raw:
## Summary
Three files created: utils.svh, tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh,
tb_tage_manual.sv. Makefile updated with sim_tage_manual target.
make sim_tage_manual passes with zero warnings and zero errors.
round_trip_sanity reports PASS.

## What was delivered
- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/utils.svh
    Shared utility tasks: tb_msg, tb_info, tb_warn, tb_error,
    tb_pf, start_test, stop_test, assert_reset, terminate.
    All tasks automatic. tb_error does not increment any counter.

- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh
    tage_ram_entry_t packed struct at MAX_* widths (16 bits T1-T4).
    Field 'useful' used in place of reserved word 'use'.
    Tasks: tage_ram_write, tage_ram_read, tage_set_pred_inp,
    tage_set_upd_inp, tage_predict, tage_update,
    tage_check_pred_meta, tage_round_trip_sanity.
    Hierarchical RAM paths match tage.sv generate block structure.

- rtl/core/frontend/bpu/tb/tb_tage_manual.sv
    Module tb (no ports). Section order follows manual_tb_decisions.md
    exactly. Staging FF pattern (stg_pred_val/inp, stg_upd_val/inp)
    used for struct-array ports to satisfy Verilator nba_sequent
    requirement. DUT tieoffs in dedicated initial block.
    en_round_trip=1 gates tage_round_trip_sanity.

- Makefile updated
    sim_tage_manual target added after sim_tage_tasks.
    Flags: -Wno-VARHIDDEN -Wno-UNDRIVEN -Wno-SYNCASYNCNET
    -Wno-PINMISSING -Wno-WIDTHTRUNC -I$(TB_DIR).
    Runs with +TAGE_FAST_INIT=1.

## Test Case Results
round_trip_sanity: PASS
  - T1 RAM write at idx=1165 (bank=1, row=141) succeeded.
  - RAM read-back verified all five fields (valid, ctr, useful,
    epc, tag) with no mismatches.
  - First prediction: tage_pred_rdy_p2[0] asserted, meta captured.
  - Update applied with resolved_taken=1, cond_mispredict=0.
  - Second prediction: tage_pred_rdy_p2[0] asserted.

## Assumptions made not explicit in the prompt
- -I$(TB_DIR) added to sim_tage_manual to resolve include paths
  for utils.svh and tb_tage_manual_tasks.svh. No other include
  path mechanism is used in the existing Makefile targets.
- Staging always_ff blocks placed in the Testbench Logic section
  (not the DUT Signal Logic section) since they are sequential
  blocks, not combinatorial tieoffs.
- tage_check_pred_meta takes a slot mask (logic [1:0]) to select
  which slots to compare. Consistent with existing tage_check_meta
  signature in tb_tage_tasks.sv.

## Decisions made not explicit in the prompt
- sim_tage_manual target not added to the 'all' target. Prompt
  specifies adding the target; inclusion in 'all' was not requested.
- TEST_PC=40'h0000_1234, TEST_IDX=1165, tag=8'h02 derived from the
  same hash logic used in the validated prior testbench. These are
  architectural constants for T1 with folded_hist=0, not test-case
  logic.
- tage_round_trip_sanity uses ctr=3'b110 (strong taken) to ensure
  the T1 hit drives a definite prediction direction.
- err_round_trip and en_round_trip declared at module scope (not
  inside initial) so they are visible to run_tests task.
- test_name declared as string at module scope per spec so it is

## Task ID
TOOLS-001

## Hypothesis
The coverage script can be updated to handle known shared encodings
without changing its core string-matching approach. A known-exceptions
table mapping instruction mnemonics to their shared encoding equivalents
will eliminate false negatives while remaining easy to extend as new
shared encodings are discovered.

---

## Background
DECODE-003 identified that check_rva23_coverage.py reports Zcb as 12/13
because c.sext.w has no label in rvc_expander.sv. The instruction is
correctly handled via the shared C.ADDIW path (Q1/3'b001, rd!=0, imm=0).
The script has no mechanism to handle this case.

A second known gap in the tools/riscv-opcodes/extensions/rv_zcb file was
also found - c.zext.w is absent from the tools file but is present in the
published Zcb spec and correctly implemented. The script needs to handle
this case too.

## Specific Requirements

1. Read tools/check_rva23_coverage.py in full before making any changes.
   Understand the current matching approach completely.

2. Add a KNOWN_SHARED_ENCODINGS table near the top of the script:
   - Format: dict mapping missing mnemonic to the RTL label that
     handles it
   - When a mnemonic is not found by string match, check this table
   - If the equivalent label IS found in RTL, mark as covered with
     a note: covered via shared encoding <label>
   - Example entry:
       c.sext.w  -> C.ADDIW  (shared encoding, imm=0 case)

3. Add a KNOWN_OPCODES_FILE_GAPS table near the top of the script:
   - Format: dict mapping mnemonic to its expansion and source
   - These are instructions absent from riscv-opcodes files but
     present in the ratified spec and correctly implemented
   - Mark these as covered with a note: covered per spec, absent
     from riscv-opcodes tools file
   - Example entry:
       c.zext.w  -> ADD.UW rd,rd,x0  (absent from rv_zcb tools file)

4. Both tables must have a comment block explaining:
   - Why the table exists
   - How to add new entries
   - Reference to the experiment that identified each entry

5. Re-run the script after changes and confirm:
   - Zcb reports 13/13
   - No other extension coverage numbers change
   - Total coverage numbers update correctly

6. The script must still run with standard Python 3, no extra packages.
   ASCII only in all output - no Unicode characters.

---

## Constraints
- Changes confined to tools/check_rva23_coverage.py only
- Do not change the core string matching approach
- Both tables must be easy to extend - clear format, good comments
- Script output must clearly distinguish between:
    COVERED     - found by direct string match
    COVERED*    - found via shared encoding or spec exception
    MISSING     - genuinely not handled

---

## Deliverables
1. Updated tools/check_rva23_coverage.py with both exception tables
2. Re-run output showing Zcb at 13/13
3. Full coverage summary showing updated totals
4. Note any other false negatives discovered during the fix
5. Confirm no coverage numbers decreased from DECODE-003 baseline
